section.title: The Centurion, the Widow's Son, and the Sinful Woman
section.sectionRationale: Four scenes answer the question Luke's readers are asking: who is this Jesus? A Gentile centurion shows the greatest faith Jesus has found in Israel; at Nain Jesus halts a funeral and raises a widow's only son; He answers John the Baptist's doubt by pointing to His works and testifies to John's greatness; and at a Pharisee's table a sinful woman washes His feet with her tears while He pronounces her sins forgiven. Together they display the Savior for the outsider, the grieving, and the sinner.
section.thesis: Jesus is the promised One whose word heals across every boundary, whose compassion raises the dead, and whose authority forgives sins; the faith that amazes Him is found in a Gentile soldier and a weeping sinner, not in the religiously respectable, and to such faith He says, go in peace.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the centurion whose faith amazes Jesus; the raising of the widow's son at Nain; John's question and Jesus' testimony about John; and a sinful woman forgiven at Simon the Pharisee's table.

units.label: The faith of the centurion
units.note: such great faith, not found in Israel

verses.text: When Jesus had concluded His discourse in the hearing of the people, He went to Capernaum.
There a highly valued servant of a centurion was sick and about to die.
When the centurion heard about Jesus, he sent some Jewish elders to ask Him to come and heal his servant.
They came to Jesus and pleaded with Him earnestly, “This man is worthy to have You grant this,
for he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.”
So Jesus went with them. But when He was not far from the house, the centurion sent friends with the message: “Lord, do not trouble Yourself, for I am not worthy to have You come under my roof.
That is why I did not consider myself worthy to come to You. But just say the word, and my servant will be healed.
For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell one to go, and he goes, and another to come, and he comes. I tell my servant to do something, and he does it.”
When Jesus heard this, He marveled at the centurion. Turning to the crowd following Him, He said, “I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such great {{pistis|faith}}.”
And when the messengers returned to the house, they found the servant in good health.

units.label: The widow's son at Nain
units.note: the Lord had compassion and raised the dead

verses.text: Soon afterward, Jesus went to a town called Nain. His disciples went with Him, accompanied by a large crowd.
As He approached the town gate, He saw a dead man being carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow. And a large crowd from the town was with her.
When the Lord saw her, He had {{splanchnizomai|compassion}} on her and said, “Do not weep.”
Then He went up and touched the coffin, and those carrying it stood still. “Young man,” He said, “I tell you, get up!”
And the dead man sat up and began to speak! Then Jesus gave him back to his mother.
A sense of awe swept over all of them, and they glorified God. “A great prophet has appeared among us!” they said. “God has visited His people!”
And the news about Jesus spread throughout Judea and all the surrounding region.

units.label: John's question and Jesus' testimony
units.note: are You the One who was to come

verses.text: Then John’s disciples informed him about all these things.
So John called two of his disciples and sent them to ask the Lord, “Are You the One who was to come, or should we look for someone else?”
When the men came to Jesus, they said, “John the Baptist sent us to ask, ‘Are You the One who was to come, or should we look for someone else?’”
At that very hour Jesus healed many people of their diseases, afflictions, and evil spirits, and He gave sight to many who were blind.

So He replied, “Go back and report to John what you have seen and heard: The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor.
Blessed is the one who does not fall away on account of Me.”
After John’s messengers had left, Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John: “What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed swaying in the wind?
Otherwise, what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? Look, those who wear elegant clothing and live in luxury are found in palaces.
What then did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet.
This is the one about whom it is written: ‘Behold, I will send My messenger ahead of You, who will prepare Your way before You.’
I tell you, among those born of women there is no one greater than John, yet even the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.”
All the people who heard this, even the tax collectors, acknowledged God’s justice. For they had received the baptism of John.
But the Pharisees and experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves, because they had not been baptized by John.
“To what, then, can I compare the men of this generation? What are they like?
They are like children sitting in the marketplace and calling out to one another: ‘We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not weep.’
For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, ‘He has a demon!’
The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and you say, ‘Look at this glutton and drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!’
But wisdom is vindicated by all her children.”

units.label: A sinful woman forgiven
units.note: her many sins are forgiven, for she loved much

verses.text: Then one of the Pharisees invited Jesus to eat with him, and He entered the Pharisee’s house and reclined at the table.
When a sinful woman from that town learned that Jesus was dining there, she brought an alabaster jar of perfume.
As she stood behind Him at His feet weeping, she began to wet His feet with her tears and wipe them with her hair. Then she kissed His feet and anointed them with the perfume.
When the Pharisee who had invited Jesus saw this, he said to himself, “If this man were a prophet, He would know who this is and what kind of woman is touching Him, for she is a sinner!”
But Jesus answered him, “Simon, I have something to tell you.” “Tell me, Teacher,” he said.
“Two men were debtors to a certain moneylender. One owed him five hundred denarii, and the other fifty.
When they were unable to repay him, he forgave both of them. Which one, then, will love him more?”
“I suppose the one who was forgiven more,” Simon replied. “You have judged correctly,” Jesus said.

And turning toward the woman, He said to Simon, “Do you see this woman? When I entered your house, you did not give Me water for My feet, but she wet My feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.
You did not greet Me with a kiss, but she has not stopped kissing My feet since I arrived.
You did not anoint My head with oil, but she has anointed My feet with perfume.
Therefore I tell you, her many sins have been forgiven, for she has loved much. But he who has been forgiven little loves little.”
Then Jesus said to her, “Your sins are {{aphiemi|forgiven}}.”
But those at the table began to say to themselves, “Who is this who even forgives sins?”
And Jesus told the woman, “Your faith has {{sozo|saved}} you; go in peace.”

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, a confident reliance that takes God at His word. The centurion's faith amazes Jesus because it rests entirely on the power of Jesus' bare word, trusting that He need only speak and it is done. The same word crowns the chapter as Jesus tells the forgiven woman that her faith has saved her.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion from deep within, literally to feel it in the inward parts. It describes a visceral, gut-level mercy. At Nain the Lord sees a widow burying her only son and is moved with this compassion, and He acts before she asks, halting the funeral and giving her son back alive.

aphiemi.gloss: To send away, release, cancel, or forgive. Used of releasing a debt and of forgiving sins, it pictures guilt lifted and carried off. Jesus says the woman's many sins have been sent away, and then speaks directly to her, 'Your sins are forgiven,' an authority the table guests recognize belongs to God alone.

sozo.gloss: To save, rescue, make whole. It covers deliverance from danger and disease and the fuller salvation of the whole person. To the weeping, forgiven woman Jesus says, 'Your faith has saved you; go in peace,' so that her rescue is not merely emotional relief but true salvation received by faith.

basic.context: Four scenes press the question of who Jesus is. A Roman centurion, a Gentile, sends word that Jesus need only speak and his servant will be healed, and Jesus marvels at faith greater than any in Israel. At Nain He meets a widow following her only son's coffin, is moved with compassion, and raises the young man. When John the Baptist, in prison and wavering, sends to ask if Jesus is really the One, Jesus points to His works of mercy and honors John as the greatest of the prophets. Finally, at a Pharisee's dinner, an immoral woman weeps over Jesus' feet, and He declares her many sins forgiven and tells her that her faith has saved her.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these scenes together as Luke's portrait of the Savior for the outsider and the sinner. The centurion and the sinful woman, both on the margins, show the faith that pleases Jesus, while the respectable Simon and the doubting religious leaders miss it. The parable of the two debtors interprets the woman's tears: great love flows from knowing you have been forgiven much, not the other way around.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on faith, pistis, the trust that takes Jesus at His word, praised in a Gentile soldier and in a weeping woman. At Nain Jesus is moved with splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion. To the woman He speaks release, aphiemi, her sins forgiven and sent away, and He tells her that her faith has sozo, saved her.
basic.typology: Raising the widow's only son at a town gate recalls Elijah and Elisha raising sons for grieving mothers, marking Jesus as the great prophet, yet greater, for He commands the dead by His own word. John the Baptist fulfills Malachi's promised messenger who prepares the way, and Jesus' list of works answers Isaiah's picture of the age of salvation.
basic.christ: Jesus heals a Gentile's servant from a distance by His word, raises the dead by His touch, and forgives sins with an authority that belongs to God alone. He is more than a prophet; He is the One who was to come, whose works fulfill Isaiah and whose mercy reaches the soldier, the widow, and the sinner. In forgiving the woman He does openly what only God can do.
basic.god: God visits His people in Jesus, as the crowd at Nain rightly senses. He is the God whose purpose the humble receive and the proud reject, who justifies the tax collectors and sinners who come to Him, and who cancels debts no one could repay. His mercy runs toward those who know their need.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 17 and 2 Kings 4 tell of prophets raising widows' sons. Malachi 3:1 is quoted of John the messenger. Isaiah 35 and 61 stand behind Jesus' list of works. And Luke 5:20-21 already raised the question of who can forgive sins but God alone.
basic.oneStory: The One promised through the prophets has come, and He is the Savior for the outsider and the sinner. His word crosses every boundary to heal, His compassion overturns death, and His authority forgives the guilty. The faith that receives Him is found not in the respectable but in a Gentile soldier and a weeping woman, and to such He says, go in peace.

context.literary: The four episodes are bound together by the question 'Who is this?' that the raising at Nain and the forgiving of the woman both provoke. Luke arranges them to move from a Gentile's faith, to power over death, to Jesus' own testimony about His identity and mission, and finally to a vivid enacted parable of grace at Simon's table, which draws out the theme of forgiveness and love.
context.historical: A centurion commanded around a hundred soldiers; this one is a God-fearing Gentile who has funded a synagogue, yet still counts himself unworthy. Nain was a small Galilean village, and for a widow the death of an only son meant the loss of her protector and provider. Reclining at a meal left the feet exposed behind the guests, which is how the woman could reach Jesus' feet; a host would normally offer water, a kiss, and oil, courtesies Simon withheld.
context.canonical: The centurion prefigures the ingathering of the Gentiles that Luke will trace into Acts. The raising at Nain sets Jesus in the line of Elijah and Elisha while surpassing them. Jesus' words about John close the era of prophetic preparation, and the forgiveness of the sinful woman anticipates the gospel's reach to the lost that fills Luke 15 and beyond.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer who Jesus is by showing His authority over sickness, death, and sin, and to display the faith that pleases Him in a Gentile and a sinner, over against the unbelief of the religiously secure, so that readers will trust Him and receive His forgiveness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe what Jesus did and reveal who He is; they are not promises that He will always heal or raise on demand. The abiding truths are His identity as the promised Savior, the nature of saving faith that rests on His word, and the link between being forgiven much and loving much.

debates.question: Why did John the Baptist, who had pointed to Jesus, now ask if He was the One?

views.label: Genuine wavering in prison
views.case: John languishes in Herod's prison and Jesus' ministry does not look like the fiery judgment John announced; his question voices a real, honest struggle, which Jesus answers gently with evidence rather than rebuke.

views.label: Asking for his disciples' sake
views.case: Some suggest John sent his followers so that they, not John, would hear Jesus' answer and be directed to Him; the question guides the messengers to see the works for themselves.

debates.bearing: Either way Jesus points to His works of mercy that fulfill Isaiah, and blesses those who do not fall away over Him, meeting doubt with evidence and reassurance.

debates.question: Does the woman's love earn her forgiveness, or flow from it?

views.label: Love flows from forgiveness
views.case: The parable of the two debtors makes forgiveness the cause and love the response: the one forgiven more loves more; her tears are the overflow of grace already received, and Jesus finally says her faith, not her love, has saved her.

views.label: Her love shows she was forgiven
views.case: The phrase 'for she loved much' can read as evidence rather than cause; her lavish love is the visible proof that her many sins have already been forgiven.

debates.bearing: Both guard the gospel: forgiveness is received by faith, not purchased by love, and great love is the sure fruit of knowing you have been forgiven much.

study.originalLanguages: The movement is threaded with faith, pistis, the trust that simply takes Jesus at His word. A Gentile centurion has it so fully that he asks only for a spoken command, and Jesus marvels that He has found nothing like it in Israel; the same word closes the chapter when Jesus tells the forgiven woman her faith has saved her. At Nain the Lord is moved with splanchnizomai, a compassion felt in the depths, and acts before He is asked. And at Simon's table Jesus speaks aphiemi over the woman, her sins released and carried away, then declares that her faith has sozo, saved her, and sends her away in peace. The soldier and the sinner receive what the respectable miss.

typology.type: Raising the widow's only son at Nain
typology.fulfillment: Jesus fulfills and surpasses Elijah and Elisha, who raised widows' sons, 1 Kings 17:17-24 and 2 Kings 4:32-37, for He raises the dead by His own word.

typology.type: John the Baptist as the messenger
typology.fulfillment: John fulfills Malachi 3:1, the messenger sent to prepare the way of the Lord, the greatest of the prophets who points to Christ.

typology.type: The works Jesus lists to John
typology.fulfillment: The blind seeing, the lame walking, and good news to the poor fulfill the age of salvation promised in Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1.

study.christ: Every scene reveals Jesus. He heals a Gentile's dying servant by a word spoken at a distance, showing an authority the centurion rightly compares to a commander's. He halts a funeral and raises a dead man simply by speaking, and the crowd confesses that God has visited His people. When John wavers, Jesus identifies Himself as the One who was to come by pointing to the works Isaiah foretold. And at Simon's table He does what the guests know only God can do: He forgives sins, and He tells the woman her faith has saved her. He is the promised Savior whose mercy reaches the outsider, the grieving, and the guilty.
study.god: God is the one who visits and redeems His people, and the crowd at Nain names it exactly. He is the God whose saving purpose the humble embrace and the proud refuse, who is vindicated by the tax collectors and sinners who receive Him. Above all He is the God who forgives, cancelling debts that could never be repaid, and Jesus exercises that very divine prerogative, showing that in Him God Himself has drawn near to save.

crossReferences.note: Elijah raises the widow of Zarephath's son, the pattern Jesus fulfills and surpasses at Nain.
The promised messenger who prepares the way, which Jesus applies to John the Baptist.
Good news preached to the poor and freedom for captives, echoed in Jesus' answer to John.
The earlier scene where Jesus forgives sins and the scribes ask who can forgive but God alone, the same question raised again here.

study.oneStory: The One the prophets promised has come, and He is the Savior for the outsider and the sinner. His word heals a Gentile's servant across every boundary, His compassion raises a widow's son from death, and His authority forgives a sinful woman what she could never repay. The faith that receives Him is found not in the religiously secure but in a soldier and a weeping woman, and to such He says, go in peace. The story moves on toward the cross where this forgiving Lord will pay the debt Himself.

sermon.title: The Sinful Woman
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Luke, this message on Luke 7:36 walks through the sinful woman who anointed Jesus' feet, showing how forgiveness received produces overflowing love, and that only Jesus can tell us our sins are forgiven. A faithful, expository treatment of this movement's climactic scene.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus marvel at the centurion?

options.text: Because his faith rested wholly on Jesus' word, a greater faith than Jesus had found in Israel.
options.note: Luke 7:9 records Jesus' amazement at the centurion's trust that Jesus need only speak.

options.text: Because the centurion had built a synagogue and loved the Jewish nation.
options.note: True of the man, and cited by the elders, but Jesus marveled specifically at his faith, not his good works.

options.text: Because he was a powerful Roman officer worth honoring.
options.note: His rank is not the point; the centurion himself felt unworthy, and Jesus praised his faith.

options.text: Because he demanded that Jesus prove Himself.
options.note: The centurion did the opposite, humbly trusting Jesus' word without needing Him to come.

quiz.q: What moved Jesus to raise the widow's son at Nain?

options.text: His deep compassion for the grieving widow, whom He comforted and whose son He gave back alive.
options.note: Luke 7:13 says the Lord had compassion on her and acted before being asked.

options.text: The large crowd accompanying the funeral procession.
options.note: A crowd was present, but Jesus was moved by compassion for the widow, not by the audience.

options.text: A request from the widow for a miracle.
options.note: She did not ask; Jesus acted first, out of His own compassion.

options.text: A desire to prove He was more powerful than Herod.
options.note: The scene is about mercy and Jesus' identity, not a contest with Herod.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer John the Baptist's question about His identity?

options.text: By pointing to His works: the blind see, the lame walk, the dead are raised, and the poor hear good news.
options.note: Luke 7:22 has Jesus cite the Isaiah-shaped works of salvation as His answer.

options.text: By simply declaring that He was the Messiah.
options.note: He does affirm His identity, but He does it by pointing to the works that fulfill Isaiah, not by a bare claim.

options.text: By rebuking John sharply for his doubt.
options.note: Jesus does not rebuke John; He answers gently and then praises John as more than a prophet.

options.text: By refusing to answer the question at all.
options.note: Jesus gives a clear, evidence-based answer to John's messengers.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the two debtors teach about the sinful woman?

options.text: The one forgiven a greater debt loves more, so her great love flows from being forgiven much.
options.note: Luke 7:41-47 makes forgiveness the cause and love the grateful response.

options.text: Her lavish love was the visible proof that she had already been forgiven.
options.note: A fair reading of 'she loved much' as evidence, but the parable's logic still puts forgiveness first as the cause.

options.text: Her love earned her forgiveness by outweighing her sins.
options.note: This reverses the parable; Jesus finally says her faith, not her love, saved her.

options.text: She was forgiven because she was less sinful than Simon thought.
options.note: Jesus calls her sins many; she is forgiven much, not shown to be a minor sinner.

quiz.q: What did Jesus finally say had saved the woman?

options.text: Her faith; 'Your faith has saved you; go in peace.'
options.note: Luke 7:50 grounds her salvation in faith, received by trusting Jesus.

options.text: Her tears and her costly perfume poured on His feet.
options.note: These expressed her devotion, but Jesus names faith, not her acts, as what saved her.

options.text: Her willingness to face the scorn of the onlookers.
options.note: Her boldness was real, but it was faith in Jesus, not her courage, that saved her.

options.text: Simon's willingness to host Jesus at his table.
options.note: Simon is contrasted with the woman; his hospitality did nothing to save her.

questions: The centurion trusted that Jesus need only speak the word. Where do you need to rest more simply on what Jesus has said, rather than on what you can see or manage?
At Nain Jesus was moved with compassion for a grieving widow and acted before she asked. How does it comfort you to know that Jesus sees your grief and cares deeply about it?
John the Baptist, faithful yet struggling in prison, honestly brought his doubt to Jesus. What doubt or question do you need to bring to Jesus rather than carry alone?
The woman loved much because she had been forgiven much. Does your love for Christ reflect a deep sense of how much you have been forgiven, and where might that sense have grown cold?
Simon judged the woman while missing his own need; Jesus welcomed her. Where are you tempted to look down on 'sinners' rather than to see yourself as one who has also been forgiven?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered coffin is literally a bier, an open plank or frame on which the body was carried, so Jesus touches the funeral bier rather than a closed casket.
A denarius was a day's wage for a laborer, so five hundred denarii was well over a year's pay; the sizes of the two debts sharpen the point about forgiveness and love.

The phrase 'her many sins have been forgiven, for she has loved much' is read alongside the parable, where forgiveness is the cause and love the response, so that faith, named in verse 50, is what saves.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 7:1-50. Headings, line breaks, footnote and poem markers were skipped when building verse text. One em dash (verse 39) was converted to a comma. No dash characters remain.
